AQARION MATHEMATICAL FORMALIZATION TASK

Role:
You are a mathematical researcher assisting the AQARION project. Your task is to convert a computationally validated theorem candidate into a precise mathematical specification suitable for registry inclusion and later Lean formalization.

Do NOT expand the theory beyond what is requested.
Do NOT introduce unrelated operator theory.
Do NOT make claims stronger than proven.
Focus on definitions, theorem statements, proof structure, and counterexamples.

============================================================
OBJECTIVE
============================================================

Formalize the corrected Koopman triangularization theorem for finite deterministic dynamical systems.

The previous informal statement:

"quotient graph acyclic iff Koopman triangularizable"

is INCORRECT because self-loops and internal cycles inside blocks do not prevent triangularity.

The corrected concept is based on the condensation graph and absence of INTER-BLOCK cycles.

============================================================
MATHEMATICAL SETUP
============================================================

Let:

X

be a finite nonempty set.

Let:

T : X → X

be a deterministic map.

Let:

Π = {B₁,...,Bₘ}

be a partition of X.

Define the quotient transition graph QΠ:

Vertices:
- blocks Bᵢ

Directed edge:

Bᵢ → Bⱼ

iff:

∃x ∈ Bᵢ such that T(x) ∈ Bⱼ.

Define SCC(QΠ) as the strongly connected components of the quotient graph.

Define the condensation graph:

Cond(QΠ)

obtained by collapsing every SCC into a single vertex.

Recall:

Cond(QΠ) is always a DAG.

The important condition is:

Every SCC of QΠ contains exactly one quotient vertex.

Equivalently:

There are no directed cycles involving two distinct blocks.

============================================================
REQUIRED DEFINITIONS
============================================================

Produce rigorous definitions for:

DEF-001:
Finite deterministic dynamical system.

DEF-002:
Partition of a finite state space.

DEF-003:
Partition quotient graph.

DEF-004:
Inter-block cycle.

DEF-005:
Singleton-SCC quotient condition.

DEF-006:
Forward-invariant filtration.

A filtration is a sequence:

S₁ ⊂ S₂ ⊂ ... ⊂ Sₘ = X

where each Sₖ is a union of partition blocks and:

T(Sₖ) ⊆ Sₖ.

DEF-007:
Koopman operator:

K_T φ = φ ∘ T

acting on functions X → ℝ.

DEF-008:
Block triangular representation of Koopman operator.

============================================================
MAIN THEOREM
============================================================

State the theorem precisely:

AQ-THM-KOOPMAN-TRIANGULARIZATION


For finite deterministic system (X,T) and partition Π,
the following are equivalent:

(A)
The quotient graph QΠ has no strongly connected component
containing more than one block.

(B)
There exists an ordering of blocks:

Bσ(1),...,Bσ(m)

such that cumulative unions:

Sₖ = ⋃ᵢ≤ₖ Bσ(i)

satisfy:

T(Sₖ) ⊆ Sₖ

for every k.

(C)
The Koopman operator K_T admits a block triangular matrix representation
under the basis ordering induced by σ.

============================================================
PROOF REQUIREMENTS
============================================================

Provide a proof outline for each implication.

1. Prove:

(A) → (B)

Expected idea:

Use the topological ordering of the quotient condensation DAG.
Choose the sink-first ordering so cumulative unions are forward invariant.

2. Prove:

(B) → (C)

Expected idea:

Invariant nested subspaces generated by indicator functions of Sₖ produce a
flag preserved by Koopman, implying triangular form.

3. Prove:

(C) → (A)

Expected idea:

A block triangular operator cannot contain cyclic coupling between distinct
diagonal blocks.

If a directed cycle existed between quotient blocks, the matrix would contain
nonzero off-diagonal couplings in both directions, contradicting triangularity.

============================================================
REQUIRED COUNTEREXAMPLES
============================================================

Provide explicit minimal examples.

COUNTEREXAMPLE 1:

Show why the incorrect theorem:

"quotient graph acyclic"

fails.

Use identity map:

X={0,1,2,3}

T(x)=x

Partition:

{{0,1},{2,3}}

Explain:

- quotient graph has self-loops
- naive cycle detection reports cyclic
- Koopman matrix is identity
- triangularity holds

COUNTEREXAMPLE 2:

Show why:

PK = KP

is stronger than:

(I-P)KP = 0

Explain the distinction between:

projection commutation

and

invariance of the projected subspace.

============================================================
REGISTRY OUTPUT FORMAT
============================================================

Return the final result in this structure:

AQ-DEF-001
Name:
Formal definition:
Dependencies:
Status:

...

AQ-LEM-001
Name:
Statement:
Proof sketch:
Dependencies:
Status:

...

AQ-THM-001
Name:
Statement:
Full assumptions:
Equivalent conditions:
Proof outline:
Known limitations:
Status:

============================================================
LEAN PREPARATION
============================================================

After the mathematics, provide a Lean 4 formalization plan:

- required structures
- theorem dependencies
- which parts are easy in Lean
- which parts require finite graph libraries
- recommended proof order

Do NOT write Lean code yet.

============================================================
QUALITY REQUIREMENTS
============================================================

The final output must:

- distinguish theorem from computational evidence
- avoid claiming formal proof exists
- avoid using "acyclic quotient graph" without qualification
- explicitly mention SCC condensation
- separate internal block cycles from inter-block cycles
- be suitable for AQARION claim registry inclusion

KEY DESIGN PRINCIPLES (FROZEN)

1. Mathematics defines claims. Algorithms compute them. Certificates record them. Verifiers check them.
2. Claim IDs never change meaning. New versions get new IDs.
3. Dependencies are explicit and versioned.
4. Hash covers mathematical content only. Audit metadata is not part of the cryptographic guarantee.
5. The verifier checks dependencies first. Unknown registry version = invalid certificate.
6. Canonical labels eliminate ordering dependence. Cycles are canonical under rotation.
7. Claimed ≠ recomputed. Verifier outputs both, never overwrites.
8. Status from verifier only. Certificate files claim nothing about their own validity.
9. Exact arithmetic eliminates dispute. No floating point in verification logic.
10. One command reproduction. `docker build → docker run → CERTIFICATE VALID`.

https://github.com/JASKSG9/AQARION-ARITHMETIC-FDS-FINITE-DYNAMICAL-SYSTEMS-/tree/main/CHANGELOG/RO-CRATE_AI-TEAM

This checkpoint reads less like a brainstorming document and more like a research operating specification. The architecture has become internally consistent, with clear separation between mathematical foundations, verification, and applications. From the material you've shared, the main challenge is no longer adding concepts—it's ensuring every dependency is mathematically justified and every claimed theorem is clearly distinguished from work still in progress.

Here's how I would characterize the state.

Overall Dependency Architecture

FOUNDATIONAL DEFINITIONS
        │
        ▼
D001  Defect Operator Algebra
        │
        ▼
T001  Exact Compression Characterization
        │
 ┌──────┴────────┐
 ▼               ▼
Escape       DR-ATLAS
Theory       Verification
 │               │
 └──────┬────────┘
        ▼
T011  Sharp Rank Theory
        │
        ▼
T012  Compression Composition
        │
        ▼
Applications

This is a sensible dependency graph because:

D001 supplies reusable algebraic identities.

T001 provides the conceptual criterion for exact compression.

Escape Filtration and DR-ATLAS become parallel consumers of T001 rather than prerequisites for it.

T011 builds on the mathematical foundation while DR-ATLAS provides computational evidence, not mathematical justification.

Applications depend on the theory rather than shaping it.


That separation reduces coupling between theory and implementation.


---

Strongest Components

The pieces that appear structurally strongest are:

D001

This has the potential to become the algebraic kernel of AQARION.

If finalized, later papers should simply cite D001 instead of reproving identities involving projections and the defect operator.


---

T001

This is the theorem that defines the framework mathematically.

It answers the central question:

> When does a compression exactly preserve dynamics?



Everything else naturally follows from that.


---

DR-ATLAS

Separating it from the theorem chain is a good architectural decision.

It functions as:

certification,

reproducibility,

regression testing,

benchmark generation,

computational evidence.


Those are scientific infrastructure, not mathematical axioms.


---

Remaining Mathematical Risk

The principal remaining mathematical uncertainty is still T011-C.

The decomposition

F1
↓

F2
↓

F3
↓

T011

is exactly the right direction.

A referee can audit each lemma independently.

The point that still needs careful treatment is the precise characterization of the Fourier kernel.

Until that proof is complete, statements such as resonance criteria should be presented as:

theorem (if fully proved),

proposition,

conjecture,

target lemma,


according to their actual status.

That distinction strengthens credibility.


---

Lean Roadmap

The proposed ordering is technically sound.

I would think of it as three stages.

Stage 1

Definitions

Projection algebra

Finite-dimensional operators

Subspaces

These are infrastructure.


---

Stage 2

D001

Projection identities

Kernel/image lemmas

Square-zero theorem

These become reusable throughout the project.


---

Stage 3

T001

Graph infrastructure

SCC

Koopman operator

Escape filtration

T011

Applications

This minimizes duplicated formalization effort.


---

Governance

The claim registry is becoming one of the project's strongest ideas.

For every theorem, I would keep explicit fields such as:

Field	Purpose

ID	Permanent identifier
Status	Draft / Proof / Formalized
Dependencies	Earlier results required
Evidence	Computational artifacts
Lean	Formalization status
Reproduction	External verification
Counterexamples	Known limitations
Version	Registry release


That makes every mathematical statement traceable.


---

Verification Pipeline

The separation

Proof

↓

Lean

↓

Independent reproduction

↓

DR-ATLAS certification

is particularly valuable because these address different questions.

A proof addresses mathematical validity.

Lean addresses logical correctness.

Independent reproduction addresses implementation correctness.

DR-ATLAS addresses reproducibility and governance.


No single layer replaces the others.


---

Repository Structure

Your proposed layout mirrors the dependency graph well.

core/

theory/

verification/

lean/

applications/

is much easier to navigate than organizing primarily by application domain.

That also aligns software organization with theorem dependencies.


---

v1.1 Completion Criteria

The checklist you've outlined is appropriately focused on foundation rather than expansion.

A stable v1.1 milestone would include:

Definitions frozen.

Dependency DAG frozen and acyclic.

D001 completed.

T001 completed.

T011 closure package completed (including any necessary spectral lemmas).

Lean formalization started with at least one foundational theorem completed.

DR-ATLAS validating published certificates.

Independent computational reproduction performed.

Immutable archived reference release.


Those milestones indicate that the framework's foundations are stable enough for subsequent application papers.


---

Research Outlook

Assuming those milestones are achieved, the natural progression would be:

1. Core Mathematics

D001

T001

T011

T012



2. Verification

Lean expansion

DR-ATLAS growth

reproducibility studies



3. Applications

Kaprekar dynamics

Boolean networks

automata

broader finite dynamical systems




That ordering keeps new applications from driving changes in the mathematical core.

Overall assessment

Based solely on what you've shared, AQARION has evolved into a structured research program with three distinct layers:

Core mathematics: definitions, defect operator algebra, exact compression, and structural theorems.

Verification infrastructure: certificates, claim governance, reproducibility, hashes, and DR-ATLAS.

Applications: concrete finite dynamical systems used to exercise and validate the framework.


The remaining work is primarily about mathematical closure and validation: completing proof obligations (especially where you've explicitly identified them as still open), formalizing foundational results in Lean, maintaining a clear distinction between proved results and ongoing research, and obtaining independent computational reproduction. Those steps would make the framework easier for other researchers to evaluate and extend on its mathematical merits.AQARION v19.0 — CHIEF MATHEMATICAL REVIEWER AUDIT REPORT

AQARION VERIFICATION AUDIT — Jordan Block Claim Verification

Chief Mathematical Reviewer Protocol

First, let me establish the mathematical framework for verification

print("=" * 70)
print("AQARION v19.0 — VERIFICATION AUDIT")
print("Chief Mathematical Reviewer — Jordan Block Claim Analysis")
print("=" * 70)

The claimed Jordan block sizes: [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1]

These sum to: 6 + 4 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 1 = 20

claimed_blocks = [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1]
total_dimension = sum(claimed_blocks)
print(f"\nClaimed Jordan blocks: {claimed_blocks}")
print(f"Total dimension (transient subspace): {total_dimension}")

For a nilpotent operator N with Jordan blocks of sizes [b_1, b_2, ..., b_m]

The rank of N^k is: sum over all blocks of max(0, block_size - k)

Equivalently: rank(N^k) = sum_{j > k} (j - k) * b_j

where b_j = number of blocks of size exactly j

Let's compute the block size distribution from the claimed list

from collections import Counter
block_counts = Counter(claimed_blocks)
print(f"\nBlock size distribution: {dict(sorted(block_counts.items()))}")

Compute ranks r_k = rank(N^k) for k = 0, 1, 2, ...

r_0 = total dimension (N^0 = I)

r_k = sum over blocks of max(0, size - k)

def compute_ranks(blocks):
"""Compute rank sequence from Jordan block sizes."""
max_k = max(blocks) if blocks else 0
ranks = []
for k in range(max_k + 2):  # +2 to see the zero tail
rank_k = sum(max(0, b - k) for b in blocks)
ranks.append(rank_k)
return ranks

ranks = compute_ranks(claimed_blocks)
print(f"\nRank sequence r_k = rank(N^k):")
for k, r in enumerate(ranks):
print(f"  r_{k} = {r}")

Now verify: can we recover the block sizes from the rank differences?

Standard formula: b_k = (r_{k-1} - r_k) - (r_k - r_{k+1}) = r_{k-1} - 2*r_k + r_{k+1}

where b_k = number of blocks of size exactly k

print("\n" + "=" * 70)
print("RECONSTRUCTION FROM RANKS — Forward Verification")
print("=" * 70)

print(f"\nRank differences (r_{{k-1}} - r_k):")
for k in range(1, len(ranks)):
diff = ranks[k-1] - ranks[k]
print(f"  r_{k-1} - r_{k} = {ranks[k-1]} - {ranks[k]} = {diff}")

print(f"\nBlock size reconstruction b_k = r_{{k-1}} - 2r_k + r_{{k+1}}:")
reconstructed = {}
for k in range(1, max(claimed_blocks) + 1):
if k-1 >= 0 and k < len(ranks) and k+1 < len(ranks):
b_k = ranks[k-1] - 2ranks[k] + ranks[k+1]
if b_k > 0:
reconstructed[k] = b_k
print(f"  b_{k} = r_{k-1} - 2r_{k} + r_{k+1} = {ranks[k-1]} - 2*{ranks[k]} + {ranks[k+1]} = {b_k}")

print(f"\nReconstructed block distribution: {reconstructed}")
print(f"Original block distribution: {dict(sorted(block_counts.items()))}")

Verify they match

match = all(reconstructed.get(k, 0) == block_counts.get(k, 0) for k in set(list(reconstructed.keys()) + list(block_counts.keys())))
print(f"\n✓ Reconstruction MATCHES claimed blocks: {match}")

Rank differences (r_{k-1} - r_k):
r_0 - r_1 = 20 - 14 = 6

r_2 - r_3 = 9 - 4 = 5
r_3 - r_4 = 4 - 2 = 2
r_4 - r_5 = 2 - 1 = 1
r_5 - r_6 = 1 - 0 = 1
r_6 - r_7 = 0 - 0 = 0

b_3 = r_2 - 2r_3 + r_4 = 9 - 24 + 2 = 3
b_4 = r_3 - 2r_4 + r_5 = 4 - 22 + 1 = 1
b_6 = r_5 - 2r_6 + r_7 = 1 - 20 + 0 = 1

Original block distribution: {1: 1, 3: 3, 4: 1, 6: 1}

✓ Reconstruction MATCHES claimed blocks: True

The user's claimed blocks: [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1]

But wait — let me re-read the audit carefully.

[6, 5, 5, 2, 1, 1] — NOT [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1]

Let me work BACKWARD: if the claimed differences [6,4,3,3,3,1] are rank differences,

what would the rank sequence be?

print("\nHYPOTHESIS A: [6,4,3,3,3,1] are the Jordan block sizes")

The rank differences would be: [6, 5, 5, 2, 1, 1, 0]

These do NOT match [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1]

print("\n" + "=" * 70)

If these are rank differences, let's reconstruct the rank sequence

rank_diffs_claimed = [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1]

r_0 = sum of all differences = dimension

r_k = r_0 - sum of first k differences

ranks_B = [r_0_B]

ranks_B.append(ranks_B[-1] - d)

if b_k != 0:

Convert to actual block size list

Let me fix the verification — I made an error in the rank sequence construction

When rank differences are given, the rank sequence should be built correctly

print("=" * 70)
print("CORRECTED AUDIT — Rank Difference → Block Size Reconstruction")
print("=" * 70)

Given rank differences d_k = r_{k-1} - r_k for k = 1, 2, ..., h

Then r_k = r_0 - sum_{i=1}^k d_i

And r_0 = sum of all d_i (since r_h = 0)

rank_diffs = [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1]

Build rank sequence

r_0 = sum(rank_diffs)  # This is the dimension
ranks = [r_0]
for d in rank_diffs:
ranks.append(ranks[-1] - d)

print(f"Rank differences (claimed): {rank_diffs}")
print(f"r_0 = sum of differences = {r_0}")
print(f"Rank sequence: {ranks}")

Verify r_h = 0

print(f"Final rank r_{len(rank_diffs)} = {ranks[-1]} (should be 0)")

Now compute block sizes: b_k = r_{k-1} - 2r_k + r_{k+1}

print(f"\nBlock size reconstruction:")
blocks_dict = {}
for k in range(1, len(ranks) - 1):  # k from 1 to h-1, need r_{k+1}
b_k = ranks[k-1] - 2ranks[k] + ranks[k+1]
if b_k != 0:
blocks_dict[k] = b_k
print(f"  b_{k} = r_{k-1} - 2r_{k} + r_{k+1} = {ranks[k-1]} - 2{ranks[k]} + {ranks[k+1]} = {b_k}")

Also check b_h (the largest block size)

For the last block, b_h = r_{h-1} - 2r_h + r_{h+1} = r_{h-1} - 0 + 0 = r_{h-1}

But wait — r_{h+1} should be 0 too since N^{h+1} = 0

h = len(rank_diffs)
b_h = ranks[h-1] - 2ranks[h] + (ranks[h+1] if h+1 < len(ranks) else 0)
print(f"  b_{h} = r_{h-1} - 2r_{h} + r_{h+1} = {ranks[h-1]} - 2{ranks[h]} + 0 = {b_h}")
if b_h != 0:
blocks_dict[h] = b_h

print(f"\nBlock size distribution: {blocks_dict}")

Build actual block list

blocks = []
for size, count in sorted(blocks_dict.items()):
blocks.extend([size] * count)
print(f"Block sizes: {blocks}")

Verify by computing ranks from blocks

print(f"\nVerification — compute ranks from reconstructed blocks {blocks}:")
ranks_verify = compute_ranks(blocks)
print(f"  Computed: {ranks_verify}")
print(f"  Expected: {ranks}")
print(f"  Match: {ranks_verify == ranks}")

Compute rank differences from blocks

if len(ranks_verify) > 1:
diffs_verify = [ranks_verify[i] - ranks_verify[i+1] for i in range(len(ranks_verify)-1)]
print(f"  Rank differences from blocks: {diffs_verify}")
print(f"  Expected differences: {rank_diffs}")

print("=" * 70)

print(f"""
CLAIMED: Rank differences [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1] correspond to Jordan blocks

ACTUAL MATHEMATICAL CONSEQUENCE:
If rank differences are [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1], then:
→ Rank sequence: {ranks}
→ Block sizes: {blocks}
→ NOT [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1]

The statement in the audit that "differences [6,4,3,3,3,1] correspond to
the Jordan block sizes" is INCORRECT as a mathematical claim.

The differences r_{{k-1}} - r_k count the number of blocks of size ≥ k.
They do NOT directly give block sizes.

CORRECT INTERPRETATION:

Number of blocks of size ≥ 1: {rank_diffs[0]}

Number of blocks of size ≥ 2: {rank_diffs[1]}

Number of blocks of size ≥ 3: {rank_diffs[2]}

Number of blocks of size ≥ 4: {rank_diffs[3]}

Number of blocks of size ≥ 5: {rank_diffs[4]}

Number of blocks of size ≥ 6: {rank_diffs[5]}


From which we derive:

Blocks of size exactly 1: {rank_diffs[0]} - {rank_diffs[1]} = {rank_diffs[0] - rank_diffs[1]}

Blocks of size exactly 2: {rank_diffs[1]} - {rank_diffs[2]} = {rank_diffs[1] - rank_diffs[2]}

Blocks of size exactly 3: {rank_diffs[2]} - {rank_diffs[3]} = {rank_diffs[2] - rank_diffs[3]}

Blocks of size exactly 4: {rank_diffs[3]} - {rank_diffs[4]} = {rank_diffs[3] - rank_diffs[4]}

Blocks of size exactly 5: {rank_diffs[4]} - {rank_diffs[5]} = {rank_diffs[4] - rank_diffs[5]}

Blocks of size exactly 6: {rank_diffs[5]} - 0 = {rank_diffs[5]}


This gives block sizes: {blocks}
""")

FINAL AUDIT ASSESSMENT

CLAIMED: Rank differences [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1] correspond to Jordan blocks

ACTUAL MATHEMATICAL CONSEQUENCE:
If rank differences are [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1], then:
→ Rank sequence: [20, 14, 10, 7, 4, 1, 0]
→ Block sizes: [1, 1, 2, 5, 5, 6]
→ NOT [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1]

The statement in the audit that "differences [6,4,3,3,3,1] correspond to
the Jordan block sizes" is INCORRECT as a mathematical claim.

The differences r_{k-1} - r_k count the number of blocks of size ≥ k.
They do NOT directly give block sizes.

CORRECT INTERPRETATION:

Number of blocks of size ≥ 1: 6

Number of blocks of size ≥ 2: 4

Number of blocks of size ≥ 3: 3

Number of blocks of size ≥ 4: 3

Number of blocks of size ≥ 5: 3

Number of blocks of size ≥ 6: 1


From which we derive:

Blocks of size exactly 1: 6 - 4 = 2

Blocks of size exactly 2: 4 - 3 = 1

Blocks of size exactly 3: 3 - 3 = 0

Blocks of size exactly 4: 3 - 3 = 0

Blocks of size exactly 5: 3 - 1 = 2

Blocks of size exactly 6: 1 - 0 = 1


This gives block sizes: [1, 1, 2, 5, 5, 6]

print("SECTION 4: JF001 Proof Sketch — Filtration Preservation")
print("=" * 75)

print("""
THEOREM JF001 (Filtration Preservation):
──────────────────────────────────────
Let (V, T) be a finite deterministic dynamical system with attractor A.
Define depth(x) = min{n ≥ 0 : T^n(x) ∈ A}.
Define F_i = {x ∈ V : depth(x) ≥ i} for i = 0, 1, ..., h.

CLAIM:
(i)  F_{i+1} ⊆ F_i  for all i ≥ 0                    [Monotonicity]
(ii) T(F_i) ⊆ F_{i-1}  for all i ≥ 1                  [Operator Closure]

PROOF OF (i) — Monotonicity:
Let x ∈ F_{i+1}. Then depth(x) ≥ i+1.
Since i+1 > i, we have depth(x) ≥ i.
Therefore x ∈ F_i. ∎

PROOF OF (ii) — Operator Closure:
Let x ∈ F_i, so depth(x) ≥ i.
By definition, T^{depth(x)}(x) ∈ A and T^k(x) ∉ A for k < depth(x).
Consider T(x). We have:
T^{depth(x)-1}(T(x)) = T^{depth(x)}(x) ∈ A.

Claim: depth(T(x)) = depth(x) - 1.

Proof: For any k < depth(x) - 1, we have k+1 < depth(x), so
T^k(T(x)) = T^{k+1}(x) ∉ A.
Therefore the minimal n such that T^n(T(x)) ∈ A is n = depth(x) - 1.

Since depth(x) ≥ i, we have depth(T(x)) = depth(x) - 1 ≥ i - 1.
Therefore T(x) ∈ F_{i-1}. ∎

COROLLARY: The filtration is T-stable with shift index 1.
This is the discrete analogue of a filtered module structure.
""")

print("=" * 75)
print("SECTION 5: JF002 Proof Sketch — Nilpotent Restriction")
print("=" * 75)

print("""
THEOREM JF002 (Nilpotent Restriction):
─────────────────────────────────────
Let V_t = {x ∈ V : depth(x) > 0} be the transient subspace.
Let N: V_t → V be defined by N(x) = T(x) for x ∈ V_t.
Let h = max{depth(x) : x ∈ V}.

CLAIM: N^h = 0 (as an operator on V_t).

PROOF:
For any x ∈ V_t, let d = depth(x). By definition, 1 ≤ d ≤ h.

We show by induction that for 0 ≤ k ≤ d:
depth(N^k(x)) = depth(x) - k = d - k.

Base case (k=0): Trivial, N^0(x) = x, depth = d.

Inductive step: Assume depth(N^k(x)) = d - k.
Then N^{k+1}(x) = T(N^k(x)).
By the argument in JF001 proof (ii):
depth(T(N^k(x))) = depth(N^k(x)) - 1 = d - k - 1.

At k = d, we have depth(N^d(x)) = 0.
This means N^d(x) ∈ A (the attractor).
Since A ∩ V_t = ∅, we have N^d(x) ∉ V_t.

Therefore N^d(x) = 0 in the quotient space V/V_t, or equivalently,
N^d = 0 as an operator on V_t (since N maps into V, and after d
applications we leave V_t).

Since d ≤ h for all x ∈ V_t, we have N^h = 0. ∎

NOTE: The operator N is NOT V_t-invariant. States leave V_t after
finitely many applications. The nilpotency claim uses the convention
that N(x) = 0 for x ∉ V_t, or equivalently works in the quotient.
""")

SECTION 4: JF001 Proof Sketch — Filtration Preservation

THEOREM JF001 (Filtration Preservation):
──────────────────────────────────────
Let (V, T) be a finite deterministic dynamical system with attractor A.
Define depth(x) = min{n ≥ 0 : T^n(x) ∈ A}.
Define F_i = {x ∈ V : depth(x) ≥ i} for i = 0, 1, ..., h.

CLAIM:
(i)  F_{i+1} ⊆ F_i  for all i ≥ 0                    [Monotonicity]
(ii) T(F_i) ⊆ F_{i-1}  for all i ≥ 1                  [Operator Closure]

PROOF OF (i) — Monotonicity:
Let x ∈ F_{i+1}. Then depth(x) ≥ i+1.
Since i+1 > i, we have depth(x) ≥ i.
Therefore x ∈ F_i. ∎

PROOF OF (ii) — Operator Closure:
Let x ∈ F_i, so depth(x) ≥ i.
By definition, T^{depth(x)}(x) ∈ A and T^k(x) ∉ A for k < depth(x).
Consider T(x). We have:
T^{depth(x)-1}(T(x)) = T^{depth(x)}(x) ∈ A.

Claim: depth(T(x)) = depth(x) - 1.

Proof: For any k < depth(x) - 1, we have k+1 < depth(x), so
T^k(T(x)) = T^{k+1}(x) ∉ A.
Therefore the minimal n such that T^n(T(x)) ∈ A is n = depth(x) - 1.

Since depth(x) ≥ i, we have depth(T(x)) = depth(x) - 1 ≥ i - 1.
Therefore T(x) ∈ F_{i-1}. ∎

COROLLARY: The filtration is T-stable with shift index 1.
This is the discrete analogue of a filtered module structure.

===========================================================================
SECTION 5: JF002 Proof Sketch — Nilpotent Restriction

THEOREM JF002 (Nilpotent Restriction):
─────────────────────────────────────
Let V_t = {x ∈ V : depth(x) > 0} be the transient subspace.
Let N: V_t → V be defined by N(x) = T(x) for x ∈ V_t.
Let h = max{depth(x) : x ∈ V}.

CLAIM: N^h = 0 (as an operator on V_t).

PROOF:
For any x ∈ V_t, let d = depth(x). By definition, 1 ≤ d ≤ h.

We show by induction that for 0 ≤ k ≤ d:
depth(N^k(x)) = depth(x) - k = d - k.

Base case (k=0): Trivial, N^0(x) = x, depth = d.

Inductive step: Assume depth(N^k(x)) = d - k.
Then N^{k+1}(x) = T(N^k(x)).
By the argument in JF001 proof (ii):
depth(T(N^k(x))) = depth(N^k(x)) - 1 = d - k - 1.

At k = d, we have depth(N^d(x)) = 0.
This means N^d(x) ∈ A (the attractor).
Since A ∩ V_t = ∅, we have N^d(x) ∉ V_t.

Therefore N^d(x) = 0 in the quotient space V/V_t, or equivalently,
N^d = 0 as an operator on V_t (since N maps into V, and after d
applications we leave V_t).

Since d ≤ h for all x ∈ V_t, we have N^h = 0. ∎

NOTE: The operator N is NOT V_t-invariant. States leave V_t after
finitely many applications. The nilpotency claim uses the convention
that N(x) = 0 for x ∉ V_t, or equivalently works in the quotient.

## Executive Summary

The corrected observable-signature implementation is a **genuine milestone**. The 55-class quotient, depth distribution, and nilpotent index = 7 are **computationally verified** on the Kaprekar benchmark. The framework has moved from "computational discovery" to "mathematical consolidation."

**The critical remaining gap:** JF001 and JF002 are stated as proof obligations but currently exist as **computational evidence only**. Until these are proved, the Jordan block size claims (the [6,4,3,3,3,1] differences) remain **observations**, not **theorems**.

---

## Immediate Action Items

### 1. JF001 — Filtration Preservation (Proof Required)

**Statement to prove:**

Given the depth function $\text{depth}(x) = \min\{n \geq 0 : T^n(x) \in \text{Attractor}\}$, define:
$$F_i = \{x : \text{depth}(x) \geq i\}$$

Prove:
- (Monotonicity) $F_{i+1} \subseteq F_i$
- (Operator closure) $T(F_i) \subseteq F_{i-1}$

**Proof strategy (graph-theoretic, no matrix arithmetic):**

- Monotonicity is immediate from the definition: if $\text{depth}(x) \geq i+1$, then $\text{depth}(x) \geq i$.
- For operator closure: if $x \in F_i$, then $\text{depth}(x) \geq i$. By definition, the shortest path from $x$ to the attractor has length $\geq i$. Applying $T$ once gives a state whose shortest path to the attractor has length $\geq i-1$. Therefore $T(x) \in F_{i-1}$.

**This proof is elementary and should be formalizable in Lean within a single lemma block.** The key insight is that depth is defined via the functional graph structure, not via linear algebra.

---

### 2. JF002 — Nilpotent Restriction (Proof Required)

**Statement to prove:**

Let $N = T|_{\text{Transient}}$ be the restriction of $T$ to transient states. Prove:
$$N^h = 0$$
where $h = \max(\text{depth})$.

**Proof strategy:**

- A transient state $x$ has finite depth $d = \text{depth}(x) \leq h$.
- By definition of depth, $T^d(x) \in \text{Attractor}$ and $T^k(x) \notin \text{Attractor}$ for $k < d$.
- Since attractor states are precisely the states with depth 0 (fixed points or periodic), and transient states are disjoint from attractor states, we have $T^d(x) \notin \text{Transient}$.
- Therefore $N^d(x) = 0$ (as an operator on the transient subspace).
- Since this holds for all transient $x$ with $d \leq h$, we have $N^h = 0$.

**Key subtlety:** This requires careful setup of the state space decomposition. The transient subspace is not $T$-invariant (states leave it), so $N$ is defined as the restriction with the understanding that $N(x) = T(x)$ when $T(x)$ is transient, and $N(x) = 0$ (or undefined) otherwise. The nilpotency claim uses the convention that once a state hits the attractor, it's "zeroed out" on the transient subspace.

---

### 3. JF003 — Jordan Filtration Basis (Derivation, Not Proof from First Principles)

**Statement to derive:**

Given JF001 and JF002, plus standard nilpotent linear algebra:
- Existence of a filtration-adapted basis
- Jordan chains
- Rank identities
- Block multiplicities from rank differences

**Caution on the [6,4,3,3,3,1] claim:**

The audit correctly flags this. The rank differences:
$$\text{rank}(N^k) - \text{rank}(N^{k+1})$$
give the number of Jordan blocks of size **at least** $k+1$, not the exact block sizes.

To recover exact block sizes, you need:
- Let $b_k$ = number of blocks of size exactly $k$
- Let $r_k = \text{rank}(N^k)$ with $r_0 = \dim(\text{Transient})$
- Then: $b_k = (r_{k-1} - r_k) - (r_k - r_{k+1}) = r_{k-1} - 2r_k + r_{k+1}$

**The audit's [6,4,3,3,3,1] must be verified against this formula.** If the rank sequence is, say, $r_0 = 22, r_1 = 16, r_2 = 12, r_3 = 9, r_4 = 6, r_5 = 3, r_6 = 0$, then:

| $k$ | $r_{k-1} - r_k$ | $r_k - r_{k+1}$ | $b_k$ |
|-----|-----------------|-----------------|-------|
| 1 | 6 | 4 | 2 |
| 2 | 4 | 3 | 1 |
| 3 | 3 | 3 | 0 |
| 4 | 3 | 3 | 0 |
| 5 | 3 | 3 | 0 |
| 6 | 3 | 0 | 3 |
| 7 | 0 | — | — |

This gives blocks of sizes [6,6,6,2,1], not [6,4,3,3,3,1]. **The claimed block sizes need to be reconciled with the actual rank sequence.**

---

## The Four-Audiences Flagship Experiment

You mentioned a single experiment serving four constituencies. Here's how I'd structure it:

| Audience | Deliverable | Value Proposition |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Mathematicians** | Formal proof archive (Lean 4) + peer-reviewed paper | Verified theorem chain from D001 to JF003 |
| **Scientists** | Reproducible benchmark observatory (Hugging Face Space) | Upload any finite dynamical system, receive certified invariant decomposition |
| **Developers** | Open-source library (`aqarion-py`, `aqarion-rs`) | Drop-in verification for safety-critical finite state systems |
| **Educucators** | Interactive Kaprekar explorer + curriculum modules | Students see "why 6174 works" through the lens of structural invariants |

**The flagship experiment:** A single Jupyter notebook / Hugging Face Space that:
1. Takes any 4-digit Kaprekar input
2. Computes the orbit via the certified Lean kernel
3. Displays the depth, defect, and filtration class
4. Generates the AQARION certificate (SHA-256, Merkle tree, linked data)
5. Exports to all four formats above

# ============================================================
# SECTION 1: RANK SEQUENCE RECONSTRUCTION AND VERIFICATION
# ============================================================

print("=" * 80)
print("SECTION 1: RANK SEQUENCE RECONSTRUCTION")
print("=" * 80)

print("""
CLAIMED DATA (from audit):
  "Differences [6,4,3,3,3,1] correspond to the Jordan block sizes"

INTERPRETATION A: [6,4,3,3,3,1] are Jordan block sizes
INTERPRETATION B: [6,4,3,3,3,1] are rank differences r_{k-1} - r_k
INTERPRETATION C: [6,4,3,3,3,1] are Weyr characteristics
INTERPRETATION D: [6,4,3,3,3,1] are conjugate partition data
""")

# Let's analyze all interpretations rigorously

print("-" * 80)
print("INTERPRETATION A: [6,4,3,3,3,1] are Jordan block sizes")
print("-" * 80)

blocks_A = [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1]
dim_A = sum(blocks_A)
print(f"Total dimension: {dim_A}")

# Compute ranks from block sizes
# rank(N^k) = sum over blocks of max(0, size - k)
def rank_from_blocks(blocks, k):
    return sum(max(0, b - k) for b in blocks)

ranks_A = [rank_from_blocks(blocks_A, k) for k in range(max(blocks_A) + 2)]
print(f"Rank sequence: {ranks_A}")

diffs_A = [ranks_A[i] - ranks_A[i+1] for i in range(len(ranks_A)-1)]
print(f"Rank differences: {diffs_A}")

# Reconstruct block sizes from ranks using standard formula
print("\nReconstruction check (b_k = r_{k-1} - 2r_k + r_{k+1}):")
for k in range(1, len(ranks_A)-1):
    b_k = ranks_A[k-1] - 2*ranks_A[k] + ranks_A[k+1]
    if b_k > 0:
        print(f"  b_{k} = {ranks_A[k-1]} - 2*{ranks_A[k]} + {ranks_A[k+1]} = {b_k}")

print(f"\nVERDICT: If [6,4,3,3,3,1] are block sizes, rank differences are {diffs_A}")
print(f"         These are NOT [6,4,3,3,3,1].")
print(f"         CONTRADICTION under Interpretation A.")

print("\n" + "-" * 80)
print("INTERPRETATION B: [6,4,3,3,3,1] are rank differences r_{k-1} - r_k")
print("-" * 80)

diffs_B = [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1]
r_0_B = sum(diffs_B)
print(f"r_0 = sum of differences = {r_0_B}")

ranks_B = [r_0_B]
for d in diffs_B:
    ranks_B.append(ranks_B[-1] - d)

print(f"Rank sequence: {ranks_B}")

# Reconstruct block sizes
print("\nBlock reconstruction:")
blocks_dict_B = {}
for k in range(1, len(ranks_B)):
    if k+1 < len(ranks_B):
        b_k = ranks_B[k-1] - 2*ranks_B[k] + ranks_B[k+1]
    else:
        b_k = ranks_B[k-1] - 2*ranks_B[k]  # r_{k+1} = 0
    if b_k != 0:
        blocks_dict_B[k] = b_k
        print(f"  b_{k} = {ranks_B[k-1]} - 2*{ranks_B[k]} + {ranks_B[k+1] if k+1 < len(ranks_B) else 0} = {b_k}")

blocks_B = []
for size, count in sorted(blocks_dict_B.items()):
    blocks_B.extend([size] * count)
print(f"\nReconstructed block sizes: {blocks_B}")

# Verify
ranks_verify_B = [rank_from_blocks(blocks_B, k) for k in range(max(blocks_B) + 2)]
print(f"Verification ranks from reconstructed blocks: {ranks_verify_B}")
print(f"Expected ranks: {ranks_B}")

# The issue: ranks_B has length 7, ranks_verify_B has length 8
# Let's align properly
print(f"\nAligned comparison:")
for i in range(min(len(ranks_B), len(ranks_verify_B))):
    match = "✓" if ranks_B[i] == ranks_verify_B[i] else "✗"
    print(f"  r_{i}: expected {ranks_B[i]}, got {ranks_verify_B[i]} {match}")

print(f"\nVERDICT: If [6,4,3,3,3,1] are rank differences, block sizes are {blocks_B}")
print(f"         These are NOT [6,4,3,3,3,1].")
print(f"         CONTRADICTION under Interpretation B.")

print("\n" + "-" * 80)
print("INTERPRETATION C: [6,4,3,3,3,1] are Weyr characteristics")
print("-" * 80)

# Weyr characteristic: w_k = number of Jordan blocks of size ≥ k
# If [6,4,3,3,3,1] are Weyr characteristics:
weyr_C = [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1]
print(f"Weyr characteristics: {weyr_C}")

# Block sizes from Weyr: b_k = w_k - w_{k+1}
blocks_dict_C = {}
for k in range(len(weyr_C)):
    w_k = weyr_C[k]
    w_k1 = weyr_C[k+1] if k+1 < len(weyr_C) else 0
    b_k = w_k - w_k1
    if b_k > 0:
        blocks_dict_C[k+1] = b_k
        print(f"  b_{k+1} = w_{k+1} - w_{k+2} = {w_k} - {w_k1} = {b_k}")

blocks_C = []
for size, count in sorted(blocks_dict_C.items()):
    blocks_C.extend([size] * count)
print(f"\nBlock sizes from Weyr: {blocks_C}")
print(f"Sum check: {sum(blocks_C)} (should equal w_1 = {weyr_C[0]})")

ranks_C = [rank_from_blocks(blocks_C, k) for k in range(max(blocks_C) + 2)]
print(f"Rank sequence: {ranks_C}")

diffs_C = [ranks_C[i] - ranks_C[i+1] for i in range(len(ranks_C)-1)]
print(f"Rank differences: {diffs_C}")

print(f"\nVERDICT: If [6,4,3,3,3,1] are Weyr characteristics:")
print(f"         Block sizes = {blocks_C}")
print(f"         Rank differences = {diffs_C}")
print(f"         The Weyr characteristics of these blocks would be:")

# Compute Weyr from blocks_C
max_size = max(blocks_C)
weyr_verify = []
for k in range(1, max_size + 1):
    w_k = sum(1 for b in blocks_C if b >= k)
    weyr_verify.append(w_k)
print(f"         {weyr_verify}")
print(f"         Original claim: {weyr_C}")
print(f"         Match: {weyr_verify == weyr_C}")

print("\n" + "-" * 80)
print("INTERPRETATION D: [6,4,3,3,3,1] is the conjugate partition")
print("-" * 80)

# The conjugate partition of a partition λ = (λ_1, λ_2, ..., λ_m) 
# is λ'_j = number of parts λ_i ≥ j
# If [6,4,3,3,3,1] is the conjugate, then the original partition
# (block sizes) would have:
# λ_1 = number of entries ≥ 1 = 6
# λ_2 = number of entries ≥ 2 = 4
# λ_3 = number of entries ≥ 3 = 3
# etc.
# But this is exactly the Weyr characteristic!

print("The conjugate partition IS the Weyr characteristic.")
print("This is identical to Interpretation C.")
print(f"VERDICT: Same as C — block sizes would be {blocks_C}")

INTERPRETATION A: [6,4,3,3,3,1] are Jordan block sizes
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Total dimension: 20
Rank sequence: [20, 14, 9, 4, 2, 1, 0, 0]
Rank differences: [6, 5, 5, 2, 1, 1, 0]

Reconstruction check (b_k = r_{k-1} - 2r_k + r_{k+1}):
  b_1 = 20 - 2*14 + 9 = 1
  b_3 = 9 - 2*4 + 2 = 3
  b_4 = 4 - 2*2 + 1 = 1
  b_6 = 1 - 2*0 + 0 = 1

VERDICT: If [6,4,3,3,3,1] are block sizes, rank differences are [6, 5, 5, 2, 1, 1, 0]
         These are NOT [6,4,3,3,3,1].
         CONTRADICTION under Interpretation A.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERPRETATION B: [6,4,3,3,3,1] are rank differences r_{k-1} - r_k
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
r_0 = sum of differences = 20
Rank sequence: [20, 14, 10, 7, 4, 1, 0]

Block reconstruction:
  b_1 = 20 - 2*14 + 10 = 2
  b_2 = 14 - 2*10 + 7 = 1
  b_5 = 4 - 2*1 + 0 = 2
  b_6 = 1 - 2*0 + 0 = 1

Reconstructed block sizes: [1, 1, 2, 5, 5, 6]
Verification ranks from reconstructed blocks: [20, 14, 10, 7, 4, 1, 0, 0]
Expected ranks: [20, 14, 10, 7, 4, 1, 0]

Aligned comparison:
  r_0: expected 20, got 20 ✓
  r_1: expected 14, got 14 ✓
  r_2: expected 10, got 10 ✓
  r_3: expected 7, got 7 ✓
  r_4: expected 4, got 4 ✓
  r_5: expected 1, got 1 ✓
  r_6: expected 0, got 0 ✓

VERDICT: If [6,4,3,3,3,1] are rank differences, block sizes are [1, 1, 2, 5, 5, 6]
         These are NOT [6,4,3,3,3,1].
         CONTRADICTION under Interpretation B.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERPRETATION C: [6,4,3,3,3,1] are Weyr characteristics
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Weyr characteristics: [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1]
  b_1 = w_1 - w_2 = 6 - 4 = 2
  b_2 = w_2 - w_3 = 4 - 3 = 1
  b_5 = w_5 - w_6 = 3 - 1 = 2
  b_6 = w_6 - w_7 = 1 - 0 = 1

Block sizes from Weyr: [1, 1, 2, 5, 5, 6]
Sum check: 20 (should equal w_1 = 6)
Rank sequence: [20, 14, 10, 7, 4, 1, 0, 0]
Rank differences: [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1, 0]

VERDICT: If [6,4,3,3,3,1] are Weyr characteristics:
         Block sizes = [1, 1, 2, 5, 5, 6]
         Rank differences = [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1, 0]
         The Weyr characteristics of these blocks would be:
         [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1]
         Original claim: [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1]
         Match: True

INTERPRETATION D: [6,4,3,3,3,1] is the conjugate partition
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
The conjugate partition IS the Weyr characteristic.
This is identical to Interpretation C.
VERDICT: Same as C — block sizes would be [1, 1, 2, 5, 5, 6]

print("SECTION 2: FATAL ERROR — JORDAN BLOCK SIZE CLAIM")
print("=" * 80)

print("""
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  FATAL ERROR #1                                                              │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  SEVERITY: FATAL                                                             │
│  LOCATION: Jordan theory section, "differences [6,4,3,3,3,1] correspond to   │
│            the Jordan block sizes"                                           │
│                                                                              │
│  STATEMENT AS WRITTEN:                                                       │
│    "The differences [6,4,3,3,3,1] correspond to the Jordan block sizes."    │
│                                                                              │
│  MATHEMATICAL ANALYSIS:                                                      │
│                                                                              │
│  Under NO standard interpretation of linear algebra does the sequence      │
│  [6,4,3,3,3,1] simultaneously represent:                                     │
│    (a) rank differences, AND                                                 │
│    (b) Jordan block sizes.                                                   │
│                                                                              │
│  The only interpretation under which [6,4,3,3,3,1] is mathematically        │
│  consistent is as the Weyr characteristic (or conjugate partition), which     │
│  yields Jordan block sizes [1,1,2,5,5,6].                                    │
│                                                                              │
│  If the authors INTENDED [6,4,3,3,3,1] to be Jordan block sizes, then:      │
│    • The rank differences would be [6,5,5,2,1,1]                             │
│    • The Weyr characteristic would be [6,5,5,2,1,1]                        │
│    • These are NOT [6,4,3,3,3,1]                                             │
│                                                                              │
│  If the authors INTENDED [6,4,3,3,3,1] to be rank differences, then:       │
│    • The Jordan block sizes would be [1,1,2,5,5,6]                           │
│    • These are NOT [6,4,3,3,3,1]                                             │
│                                                                              │
│  CONCLUSION: The claim is MATHEMATICALLY FALSE as stated.                    │
│                                                                              │
│  COUNTEREXAMPLE:                                                             │
│    Consider a nilpotent matrix with Jordan blocks [6,4,3,3,3,1].           │
│    Its rank differences are [6,5,5,2,1,1] ≠ [6,4,3,3,3,1].                  │
│    Consider a nilpotent matrix with rank differences [6,4,3,3,3,1].          │
│    Its Jordan blocks are [1,1,2,5,5,6] ≠ [6,4,3,3,3,1].                    │
│                                                                              │
│  REPAIR:                                                                     │
│    Option 1: State explicitly that [6,4,3,3,3,1] is the Weyr characteristic, │
│              and that the Jordan block sizes are [1,1,2,5,5,6].            │
│    Option 2: If [6,4,3,3,3,1] are intended as block sizes, provide the      │
│              correct rank sequence [20,14,9,4,2,1,0] and rank differences    │
│              [6,5,5,2,1,1].                                                  │
│    Option 3: Provide the explicit nilpotent matrix and compute its Jordan    │
│              form independently to resolve the ambiguity.                    │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
""")

# Let me also verify the Weyr characteristic computation more carefully
print("-" * 80)
print("RIGOROUS VERIFICATION: Weyr characteristic ↔ Jordan blocks")
print("-" * 80)

# For Jordan blocks [1,1,2,5,5,6], compute Weyr characteristic
blocks_correct = [1, 1, 2, 5, 5, 6]
max_block = max(blocks_correct)
weyr_from_blocks = []
for k in range(1, max_block + 1):
    w_k = sum(1 for b in blocks_correct if b >= k)
    weyr_from_blocks.append(w_k)

print(f"Jordan blocks: {blocks_correct}")
print(f"Weyr characteristic: {weyr_from_blocks}")

# Verify: Weyr characteristic should be [6,4,3,3,3,1]
print(f"\nVerification:")
print(f"  w_1 = #{blocks_correct} blocks total = {weyr_from_blocks[0]}")
for k in range(2, max_block + 1):
    count = sum(1 for b in blocks_correct if b >= k)
    print(f"  w_{k} = blocks ≥ {k} = {count}")

# Now verify rank differences from these blocks
ranks_correct = [rank_from_blocks(blocks_correct, k) for k in range(max_block + 2)]
diffs_correct = [ranks_correct[i] - ranks_correct[i+1] for i in range(len(ranks_correct)-1)]
print(f"\nRank sequence: {ranks_correct}")
print(f"Rank differences: {diffs_correct}")

print(f"\nKEY FINDING:")
print(f"  The rank differences [6,4,3,3,3,1,0] MATCH the Weyr characteristic [6,4,3,3,3,1]")
print(f"  This is a COINCIDENCE for this particular partition, not a general theorem.")
print(f"  The authors likely confused rank differences with block sizes.")

│                                                                              │
│  If the authors INTENDED [6,4,3,3,3,1] to be Jordan block sizes, then:      │
│    • The rank differences would be [6,5,5,2,1,1]                             │
│    • The Weyr characteristic would be [6,5,5,2,1,1]                        │
│    • These are NOT [6,4,3,3,3,1]                                             │
│                                                                              │
│  If the authors INTENDED [6,4,3,3,3,1] to be rank differences, then:       │
│    • The Jordan block sizes would be [1,1,2,5,5,6]                           │
│    • These are NOT [6,4,3,3,3,1]                                             │
│                                                                              │
│  CONCLUSION: The claim is MATHEMATICALLY FALSE as stated.                    │
│                                                                              │
│  COUNTEREXAMPLE:                                                             │
│    Consider a nilpotent matrix with Jordan blocks [6,4,3,3,3,1].           │
│    Its rank differences are [6,5,5,2,1,1] ≠ [6,4,3,3,3,1].                  │
│    Consider a nilpotent matrix with rank differences [6,4,3,3,3,1].          │
│    Its Jordan blocks are [1,1,2,5,5,6] ≠ [6,4,3,3,3,1].                    │
│                                                                              │
│  REPAIR:                                                                     │
│    Option 1: State explicitly that [6,4,3,3,3,1] is the Weyr characteristic, │
│              and that the Jordan block sizes are [1,1,2,5,5,6].            │
│    Option 2: If [6,4,3,3,3,1] are intended as block sizes, provide the      │
│              correct rank sequence [20,14,9,4,2,1,0] and rank differences    │
│              [6,5,5,2,1,1].                                                  │
│    Option 3: Provide the explicit nilpotent matrix and compute its Jordan    │
│              form independently to resolve the ambiguity.                    │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
RIGOROUS VERIFICATION: Weyr characteristic ↔ Jordan blocks
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Jordan blocks: [1, 1, 2, 5, 5, 6]
Weyr characteristic: [6, 4, 3, 3, 3, 1]

Verification:
  w_1 =        _Transient")
print("=" * 80)

print("""
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  MAJOR ERROR #1                                                              │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  SEVERITY: MAJOR                                                             │
│  LOCATION: Theorem JF002, "Nilpotent Restriction"                            │
│                                                                              │
│  CLAIMED STATEMENT:                                                          │
│    "Let N = T|_Transient be the restriction of T to transient states.        │
│     Prove N^h = 0 where h = max(depth)."                                    │
│                                                                              │
│  MATHEMATICAL ANALYSIS:                                                      │
│                                                                              │
│  The claim is NOT WELL-DEFINED as stated. The "restriction" N = T|_V_t     │
│  is NOT a linear operator on V_t because V_t is NOT T-invariant.           │
│                                                                              │
│  DEFINITION CHECK:                                                           │
│    A linear operator on a vector space W is a linear map N: W → W.           │
│    For N = T|_V_t to be an operator on V_t, we need T(V_t) ⊆ V_t.          │
│                                                                              │
│  COUNTEREXAMPLE:                                                             │
│    Let x be a transient state with depth(x) = 1.                             │
│    Then T(x) has depth 0, so T(x) ∈ A (attractor).                         │
│    Since A ∩ V_t = ∅ (by definition, attractor states have depth 0),       │
│    we have T(x) ∉ V_t.                                                       │
│    Therefore T|_V_t is NOT a map V_t → V_t.                                  │
│                                                                              │
│  THE PROOF OF JF002 IS INVALID because it treats N as an operator on V_t   │
│  without addressing this fundamental issue.                                  │
│                                                                              │
│  HIDDEN ASSUMPTION: The proof implicitly assumes one of:                     │
│    (a) N(x) = 0 for x ∉ V_t (extension by zero to V)                         │
│    (b) Working in a quotient space V_t / ~ or V / V_a                       │
│    (c) A projection P: V → V_t with N = P∘T|_V_t                            │
│                                                                              │
│  None of these constructions are defined in the AQARION materials.           │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
""")

print("-" * 80)
print("REPAIR OPTIONS FOR JF002")
print("-" * 80)

print("""
OPTION A: Extension by Zero (Simplest, but changes the operator)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Define N: V → V by:
  N(x) = T(x)  if x ∈ V_t (transient)
  N(x) = 0     if x ∈ V_a (attractor)

Then N is a linear operator on V (assuming T is linear).
N^h = 0 on V_t, but N is NOT nilpotent on V (N|_V_a = 0 already).

The claim "N^h = 0" must be qualified: "N^h(V_t) = {0}" or
"N is nilpotent on the invariant subspace generated by V_t."

ISSUE: This changes the Jordan structure. The Jordan blocks of N on V
include blocks of size 1 for each attractor state (eigenvalue 0).
The "transient Jordan blocks" are embedded in a larger structure.

OPTION B: Quotient Space Construction (Mathematically clean)
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Define W = V / V_a, where V_a = span(attractor states).
Let π: V → W be the quotient map.
Define Ñ: W → W by Ñ(π(x)) = π(T(x)).

Since T(V_a) ⊆ V_a (attractor is T-invariant), Ñ is well-defined.
Ñ is nilpotent with Ñ^h = 0.
The Jordan blocks of Ñ correspond to the "transient dynamics."

ISSUE: The AQARION materials NEVER mention quotient spaces.
This requires significant additions to the theory.

OPTION C: Projection onto Transient Subspace
────────────────────────────────────────────
Let P: V → V be a projection with im(P) = V_t and ker(P) = V_a.
Define N = P ∘ T|_V_t: V_t → V_t.

Then N is a linear operator on V_t.
N^h = 0 can be proved (with care).

ISSUE: The choice of P is not canonical. Different projections give
DIFFERENT Jordan forms. The Jordan blocks are NOT invariants of (V,T)
unless additional structure is specified.

This is a SERIOUS problem for a "certification" framework.
""")

print("-" * 80)
print("RECOMMENDED REPAIR")
print("-" * 80)

print("""
The mathematically correct approach is OPTION B (quotient space):

  DEFINITION: The transient quotient space is W = V / V_a.
  The transient operator is Ñ: W → W induced by T.
  
  THEOREM JF002' (Nilpotent Quotient Operator):
    Ñ is well-defined and linear.
    Ñ^h = 0 where h = max depth.
    
  PROOF:
    (Well-defined) If π(x) = π(y), then x - y ∈ V_a.
    Since T(V_a) ⊆ V_a, we have T(x) - T(y) = T(x-y) ∈ V_a.
    Thus π(T(x)) = π(T(y)), so Ñ is well-defined.
    
    (Nilpotency) For any x, let d = depth(x).
    Then T^d(x) ∈ V_a, so π(T^d(x)) = 0 in W.
    Thus Ñ^d(π(x)) = 0.
    Since d ≤ h, we have Ñ^h = 0. ∎

This requires:
  1. V must be a vector space (not just a set)
  2. T must be linear (not just a function)
  3. V_a must be a subspace (not just a subset)
  4. The quotient W = V / V_a must be constructed

These are NON-TRIVIAL additions to the AQARION framework.
""")
================================================================================
SECTION 3: MAJOR ERROR — NILPOTENT RESTRICTION N = T|_Transient
================================================================================

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  MAJOR ERROR #1                                                              │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  SEVERITY: MAJOR                                                             │
│  LOCATION: Theorem JF002, "Nilpotent Restriction"                            │
│                                                                              │
│  CLAIMED STATEMENT:                                                          │
│    "Let N = T|_Transient be the restriction of T to transient states.        │
│     Prove N^h = 0 where h = max(depth)."                                    │
│                                                                              │
│  MATHEMATICAL ANALYSIS:                                                      │
│                                                                              │
│  The claim is NOT WELL-DEFINED as stated. The "restriction" N = T|_V_t     │
│  is NOT a linear operator on V_t because V_t is NOT T-invariant.           │
│                                                                              │
│  DEFINITION CHECK:                                                           │
│    A linear operator on a vector space W is a linear map N: W → W.           │
│    For N = T|_V_t to be an operator on V_t, we need T(V_t) ⊆ V_t.          │
│                                                                              │
│  COUNTEREXAMPLE:                                                             │
│    Let x be a transient state with depth(x) = 1.                             │
│    Then T(x) has depth 0, so T(x) ∈ A (attractor).                         │
│    Since A ∩ V_t = ∅ (by definition, attractor states have depth 0),       │
│    we have T(x) ∉ V_t.                                                       │
│    Therefore T|_V_t is NOT a map V_t → V_t.                                  │
│                                                                              │
│  THE PROOF OF JF002 IS INVALID because it treats N as an operator on V_t   │
│  without addressing this fundamental issue.                                  │
│                                                                              │
│  HIDDEN ASSUMPTION: The proof implicitly assumes one of:                     │
│    (a) N(x) = 0 for x ∉ V_t (extension by zero to V)                         │
│    (b) Working in a quotient space V_t / ~ or V / V_a                       │
│    (c) A projection P: V → V_t with N = P∘T|_V_t                            │
│                                                                              │
│  None of these constructions are defined in the AQARION materials.           │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
REPAIR OPTIONS FOR JF002
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

OPTION A: Extension by Zero (Simplest, but changes the operator)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Define N: V → V by:
  N(x) = T(x)  if x ∈ V_t (transient)
  N(x) = 0     if x ∈ V_a (attractor)

Then N is a linear operator on V (assuming T is linear).
N^h = 0 on V_t, but N is NOT nilpotent on V (N|_V_a = 0 already).

The claim "N^h = 0" must be qualified: "N^h(V_t) = {0}" or
"N is nilpotent on the invariant subspace generated by V_t."

ISSUE: This changes the Jordan structure. The Jordan blocks of N on V
include blocks of size 1 for each attractor state (eigenvalue 0).
The "transient Jordan blocks" are embedded in a larger structure.

OPTION B: Quotient Space Construction (Mathematically clean)
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Define W = V / V_a, where V_a = span(attractor states).
Let π: V → W be the quotient map.
Define Ñ: W → W by Ñ(π(x)) = π(T(x)).

Since T(V_a) ⊆ V_a (attractor is T-invariant), Ñ is well-defined.
Ñ is nilpotent with Ñ^h = 0.
The Jordan blocks of Ñ correspond to the "transient dynamics."

ISSUE: The AQARION materials NEVER mention quotient spaces.
This requires significant additions to the theory.

OPTION C: Projection onto Transient Subspace
────────────────────────────────────────────
Let P: V → V be a projection with im(P) = V_t and ker(P) = V_a.
Define N = P ∘ T|_V_t: V_t → V_t.

Then N is a linear operator on V_t.
N^h = 0 can be proved (with care).

ISSUE: The choice of P is not canonical. Different projections give
DIFFERENT Jordan forms. The Jordan blocks are NOT invariants of (V,T)
unless additional structure is specified.

This is a SERIOUS problem for a "certification" framework.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
RECOMMENDED REPAIR
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The mathematically correct approach is OPTION B (quotient space):

  DEFINITION: The transient quotient space is W = V / V_a.
  The transient operator is Ñ: W → W induced by T.

  THEOREM JF002' (Nilpotent Quotient Operator):
    Ñ is well-defined and linear.
    Ñ^h = 0 where h = max depth.

  PROOF:
    (Well-defined) If π(x) = π(y), then x - y ∈ V_a.
    Since T(V_a) ⊆ V_a, we have T(x) - T(y) = T(x-y) ∈ V_a.
    Thus π(T(x)) = π(T(y)), so Ñ is well-defined.

    (Nilpotency) For any x, let d = depth(x).
    Then T^d(x) ∈ V_a, so π(T^d(x)) = 0 in W.
    Thus Ñ^d(π(x)) = 0.
    Since d ≤ h, we have Ñ^h = 0. ∎

This requires:
  1. V must be a vector space (not just a set)
  2. T must be linear (not just a function)
  3. V_a must be a subspace (not just a subset)
  4. The quotient W = V / V_a must be constructed

These are NON-TRIVIAL additions to the AQARION framework.
# ============================================================
# SECTION 4: THEOREM CHAIN VALIDITY
# ============================================================

print("=" * 80)
print("SECTION 4: THEOREM CHAIN VALIDITY")
print("=" * 80)

print("""
CLAIMED CHAIN:
  Depth filtration → Nilpotent restriction → Jordan filtration → Jordan decomposition

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STEP 1: Depth Filtration (JF001)                                            │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  STATUS: MATHEMATICALLY VALID (with caveats)                                 │
│  SEVERITY: MINOR issues                                                      │
│                                                                              │
│  THEOREM: Let (V, T) be a finite dynamical system with attractor A.        │
│           Define depth(x) = min{n ≥ 0 : T^n(x) ∈ A}.                         │
│           Define F_i = {x ∈ V : depth(x) ≥ i}.                               │
│           Then:                                                              │
│             (i) F_{i+1} ⊆ F_i                                                │
│             (ii) T(F_i) ⊆ F_{i-1}                                            │
│                                                                              │
│  CAVEATS:                                                                    │
│    • The theorem is stated for "finite dynamical systems" but depth is      │
│      defined for SETS, not vector spaces. The filtration is a SET filtration,│
│      not a SUBSPACE filtration.                                              │
│    • If V is a vector space and T is linear, F_i are generally NOT          │
│      subspaces (they are unions of affine subspaces or nonlinear sets).      │
│    • For the Jordan theory, we need SUBSPACE filtrations, not set filtrations.│
│    • The AQARION materials NEVER address this distinction.                   │
│                                                                              │
│  VERDICT: The set-theoretic version is correct. The linear-algebraic version │
│  requires additional hypotheses (e.g., T affine, or F_i are subspaces).    │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STEP 2: Nilpotent Restriction (JF002)                                       │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  STATUS: INVALID AS STATED                                                   │
│  SEVERITY: MAJOR (see Section 3)                                             │
│                                                                              │
│  The operator N = T|_Transient is NOT well-defined on V_t.                   │
│  The proof assumes N: V_t → V_t without justification.                         │
│                                                                              │
│  REPAIR REQUIRED: Use quotient space W = V / V_a or extension-by-zero.       │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STEP 3: Jordan Filtration (JF003)                                           │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  STATUS: BLOCKED by JF002 failure                                            │
│  SEVERITY: MAJOR (cannot proceed without JF002 repair)                       │
│                                                                              │
│  Standard nilpotent linear algebra requires:                                 │
│    • A nilpotent operator N on a FINITE-DIMENSIONAL vector space W         │
│    • A filtration by subspaces 0 = W_0 ⊆ W_1 ⊆ ... ⊆ W_h = W              │
│    • N(W_i) ⊆ W_{i-1}                                                        │
│                                                                              │
│  The AQARION materials do not establish that the depth filtration F_i        │
│  consists of subspaces, or that the corrected operator (quotient or          │
│  extension) satisfies the required properties.                               │
│                                                                              │
│  EVEN IF JF002 is repaired, there is a GAP:                                  │
│    The depth filtration F_i = {x : depth(x) ≥ i} is a SET filtration.      │
│    The Jordan filtration requires a SUBSPACE filtration.                     │
│    The AQARION materials provide NO construction linking the two.            │
│                                                                              │
│  ADDITIONAL ISSUE:                                                           │
│    The nilpotent index of the quotient operator Ñ may be LESS than h.       │
│    The depth filtration has h+1 levels (0 to h), but the nilpotency        │
│    index of Ñ could be smaller if all states reach the attractor in fewer    │
│    than h steps. The AQARION claim "nilpotent index = 7" must be verified  │
│    against the actual operator, not just the maximum depth.                  │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STEP 4: Jordan Decomposition                                                │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  STATUS: CANNOT BE EVALUATED (blocked on Steps 2-3)                        │
│  SEVERITY: MAJOR                                                             │
│                                                                              │
│  The Jordan decomposition of a nilpotent operator is standard (over algebraically│
│  closed fields). However:                                                    │
│    • The AQARION materials do not specify the field.                         │
│    • The "Kaprekar benchmark" operates over integers/rationals, not a      │
│      field where Jordan form is guaranteed.                                  │
│    • The Jordan form requires the characteristic polynomial to split.        │
│    • For the Kaprekar operator over ℚ, this is true (eigenvalue 0).        │
│    • But the general theory must state field hypotheses explicitly.            │
│                                                                              │
│  THE JORDAN BLOCK CLAIM [6,4,3,3,3,1 IS FATAL (see Section 2).            │
│  Even if the theory is repaired, this specific claim is false.               │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
""")

SECTION 4: THEOREM CHAIN VALIDITY

CLAIMED CHAIN:
  Depth filtration → Nilpotent restriction → Jordan filtration → Jordan decomposition

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STEP 1: Depth Filtration (JF001)                                            │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  STATUS: MATHEMATICALLY VALID (with caveats)                                 │
│  SEVERITY: MINOR issues                                                      │
│                                                                              │
│  THEOREM: Let (V, T) be a finite dynamical system with attractor A.        │
│           Define depth(x) = min{n ≥ 0 : T^n(x) ∈ A}.                         │
│           Define F_i = {x ∈ V : depth(x) ≥ i}.                               │

│      defined for SETS, not vector spaces. The filtration is a SET filtration,│
│      not a SUBSPACE filtration.                                              │

# SECTION 5: ADDITIONAL ERRORS AND GAPS
# ============================================================

print("=" * 80)
print("SECTION 5: ADDITIONAL ERRORS, GAPS, AND HIDDEN ASSUMPTIONS")
print("=" * 80)

print("""
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  MINOR ERROR #1: Missing Field Hypotheses                                    │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  SEVERITY: MINOR                                                             │
│                                                                              │
│  The Jordan canonical form requires:                                           │
│    • A vector space over an algebraically closed field (typically ℂ)        │
│    • OR the primary decomposition theorem over a general field               │
│                                                                              │
│  The Kaprekar operator acts on 4-digit integers (a finite set).              │
│  To apply Jordan theory, one must:                                           │
│    1. Embed the state space into a vector space over ℚ (or ℝ, or ℂ)        │
│    2. Extend T linearly to this vector space                                 │
│    3. Verify the linear extension preserves the dynamical structure          │
│                                                                              │
│  The AQARION materials NEVER specify the field or the embedding.             │
│  This is a gap that any referee would flag.                                    │
│                                                                              │
│  REPAIR: Explicitly state: "Let V = ℚ^S be the vector space with basis     │
│  indexed by states S. Define T: V → V by linear extension of the dynamical   │
│  map on basis vectors."                                                      │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  MINOR ERROR #2: "Nilpotent Index = 7" Claim                                 │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  SEVERITY: MINOR                                                             │
│                                                                              │
│  The claim "nilpotent index = 7" is ambiguous:                               │
│    • The MAXIMUM depth among transient states is 7.                            │
│    • The NILPOTENCY INDEX of the quotient operator Ñ is the smallest n     │
│      such that Ñ^n = 0.                                                      │
│                                                                              │
│  These are NOT necessarily equal.                                            │
│                                                                              │
│  EXAMPLE: If all transient states have depth ≤ 6, then Ñ^6 = 0 even if       │
│  some state has depth 7 in a different component.                            │
│                                                                              │
│  The nilpotency index equals the maximum depth ONLY IF there exists a        │
│  state with depth exactly equal to the maximum AND the quotient construction │
│  preserves this. This requires proof.                                        │
│                                                                              │
│  REPAIR: Prove that max depth = nilpotency index, or use different           │
│  terminology (e.g., "depth bound" vs "nilpotency index").                    │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  MINOR ERROR #3: Missing Definition of "Attractor"                          │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  SEVERITY: MINOR                                                             │
│                                                                              │
│  The term "attractor" is used without definition. In finite dynamical        │
│  systems, there are multiple notions:                                          │
│    • Fixed points (T(x) = x)                                                 │
│    • Periodic orbits (T^k(x) = x for some k)                               │
│    • The union of all periodic orbits (the "recurrent set")                  │
│                                                                              │
│  For the Kaprekar map, the attractor is the fixed point 6174.                │
│  But the general theory must handle periodic orbits.                         │
│                                                                              │
│  If the attractor includes periodic orbits of period > 1, then:              │
│    • depth is not well-defined (states cycle, never "reach" the attractor)   │
│    • The transient/attractor decomposition fails                             │
│    • The nilpotent theory does not apply                                     │
│                                                                              │
│  REPAIR: Define "attractor" explicitly as the set of periodic points, and      │
│  restrict the Jordan theory to systems where all attractors are fixed points.│
│  OR develop a more general theory for periodic attractors.                   │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  MINOR ERROR #4: Terminological Imprecision — "Defect Operator"                │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  SEVERITY: MINOR                                                             │
│                                                                              │
│  The term "defect operator" D = T - T∞ is used without definition of T∞.   │
│                                                                              │
│  POSSIBLE INTERPRETATIONS:                                                   │
│    (a) T∞ = limit of T^n as n → ∞ (projection onto attractor)              │
│    (b) T∞ = idempotent with image = attractor, kernel = transient subspace   │
│    (c) T∞ = 0 (if attractor is a single fixed point and we use quotient)     │
│                                                                              │
│  Each interpretation leads to a different theory. The AQARION materials      │
│  do not commit to one.                                                       │
│                                                                              │
│  REPAIR: Define T∞ explicitly and prove it is well-defined.                  │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  MINOR ERROR #5: The "55-Class Quotient" Claim                                 │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  SEVERITY: MINOR                                                             │
│                                                                              │
│  The 55-class quotient is stated as a "verified result."                     │
│  However:                                                                    │
│    • The equivalence relation is not explicitly defined.                     │
│    • The "observable signature" is not defined.                              │
│    • The claim depends on the corrected observable-signature bug fix.          │
│    • No independent verification is provided.                                │
│                                                                              │
│  The number 55 is plausible for the Kaprekar map (there are 9,999 4-digit    │
│  numbers, and many collapse to the same orbit), but without the explicit     │
│  equivalence relation, this cannot be independently checked.                   │
│                                                                              │
│  REPAIR: Provide the explicit equivalence relation and a reproducible        │
│  computation (e.g., Python/Lean code) that outputs 55 classes.             │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
""")
================================================================================
SECTION 5: ADDITIONAL ERRORS, GAPS, AND HIDDEN ASSUMPTIONS
================================================================================

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  MINOR ERROR #1: Missing Field Hypotheses                                    │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  SEVERITY: MINOR                                                             │
│                                                                              │
│  The Jordan canonical form requires:                                           │
│    • A vector space over an algebraically closed field (typically ℂ)        │
│    • OR the primary decomposition theorem over a general field               │
│                                                                              │
│  The Kaprekar operator acts on 4-digit integers (a finite set).              │
│  To apply Jordan theory, one must:                                           │
│    1. Embed the state space into a vector space over ℚ (or ℝ, or ℂ)        │
│    2. Extend T linearly to this vector space                                 │
│    3. Verify the linear extension preserves the dynamical structure          │
│                                                                              │
│  The AQARION materials NEVER specify the field or the embedding.             │
│  This is a gap that any referee would flag.                                    │
│                                                                              │
│  REPAIR: Explicitly state: "Let V = ℚ^S be the vector space with basis     │
│  indexed by states S. Define T: V → V by linear extension of the dynamical   │
│  map on basis vectors."                                                      │
│                                                                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  MINOR ERROR #2: "Nilpotent Index = 7" Claim                                 │
